immich
Security
Advisories
GHSA-hfvf-5c8x-8rc4
OIDC discovery/token/userinfo/JWKS fetches run with TLS certificate verification unconditionally disabled via allowInsecureRequests (CWE-295)
Moderate
bo0tzz
published
GHSA-hfvf-5c8x-8rc4
Jul 6, 2026
Package
npm
@immich/server
(
npm
)
Affected versions
<=latest
Patched versions
v3.0.0
Description
OIDC discovery / token / userinfo / JWKS fetches run with TLS certificate verification unconditionally disabled via
allowInsecureRequests
(CWE-295)
Venue:
https://github.com/immich-app/immich/security/advisories/new
Affected:
immich-app/immich
— all versions through HEAD (2026-04-11) that call
getClient()
in
oauth.repository.ts
Affected files:
server/src/repositories/oauth.repository.ts:3,9,128-155
(
getClient()
passes
allowInsecureRequests
to
discovery()
unconditionally)
server/src/repositories/oauth.repository.ts:116-126
(
getProfilePicture()
calls
fetch(url)
on the OIDC
picture
claim — related; covered below)
server/src/dtos/system-config.dto.ts:439-441
(
issuerUrl
has
@IsString()
only, no
@IsUrl()
)
CWE:
CWE-295 (Improper Certificate Validation), CWE-346 (Origin Validation Error)
Summary
Immich's OIDC integration builds a single
openid-client
instance for every authentication event by calling
discovery(issuerUrl, clientId, metadata, authMethod, options)
. The
options
argument is a literal object whose
execute
array contains the
openid-client
built-in
allowInsecureRequests
interceptor — unconditionally, with no feature flag, no config toggle, no
NODE_ENV
check, and no warning. According to the
openid-client
documentation,
allowInsecureRequests
disables TLS certificate verification for every HTTP request the resulting client performs: the
.well-known/openid-configuration
discovery fetch, the JWKS fetch, the
/authorize
redirect target resolution, the
/token
POST, and the
/userinfo
GET. A network-adjacent attacker (on the same LAN, the same k8s cluster, or via DNS poisoning / BGP hijack on the path between Immich and the IdP) who can observe or intercept the TCP connection to the IdP can present any TLS certificate and will be accepted without verification.
The attacker can then serve a crafted
.well-known/openid-configuration
response that points
token_endpoint
and
userinfo_endpoint
at an attacker-controlled host, a crafted
/token
response that returns a self-signed JWT, and a crafted
/userinfo
response that claims the victim is an arbitrary local Immich user (including the administrator, whose email is typically the admin's well-known address). Immich's
userMapping
logic then matches the OIDC identity against a local user and logs the attacker in with that user's session — full authentication bypass on every Immich deployment that has OIDC enabled.
Because the
allowInsecureRequests
flag is applied to the entire
openid-client
HTTP stack, this also affects any fetch the client makes to the JWKS endpoint, which is used to validate the signature of received ID tokens. An attacker who can forge the JWKS response can sign arbitrary JWTs with a key pair they control and submit them as valid ID tokens.
Details
Unconditional
allowInsecureRequests
in
getClient()
server/src/repositories/oauth.repository.ts:128-155
:
private
async
getClient
(
{
issuerUrl
,
clientId
,
clientSecret
,
profileSigningAlgorithm
,
signingAlgorithm
,
tokenEndpointAuthMethod
,
timeout
,
}
:
OAuthConfig
)
{
try
{
return
await
discovery
(
new
URL
(
issuerUrl
)
,
clientId
,
{
client_secret
:
clientSecret
,
response_types
:
[
'code'
]
,
userinfo_signed_response_alg
:
profileSigningAlgorithm
===
'none'
?
undefined
:
profileSigningAlgorithm
,
id_token_signed_response_alg
:
signingAlgorithm
,
}
,
this
.
getTokenAuthMethod
(
tokenEndpointAuthMethod
,
clientSecret
)
,
{
execute
:
[
allowInsecureRequests
]
,
// <-- unconditionally applied
timeout
,
}
,
)
;
}
catch
(
error
: any
|
AggregateError
)
{
this
.
logger
.
error
(
`Error in OAuth discovery:
${
error
}
`
,
error
?.
stack
,
error
?.
errors
)
;
throw
new
InternalServerErrorException
(
`Error in OAuth discovery:
${
error
}
`
,
{
cause
:
error
}
)
;
}
}
Observations:
The
execute
array always contains
allowInsecureRequests
. No feature flag, no
if (env.DEV)
guard, no admin-opt-in.
allowInsecureRequests
is imported from
openid-client
at line 3 of the same file as a top-level import, confirming the flag is active code, not dead code.
getClient()
is called from
authorize()
,
getProfile()
,
link()
,
unlink()
, and every OIDC-related service method that needs a client — which means every OIDC login, every profile fetch, and every token refresh runs with TLS verification disabled.
What
allowInsecureRequests
actually does (upstream
openid-client
)
The flag is a built-in execute-middleware in
openid-client
v6. It replaces the client's
customFetch
with one that sets
https.Agent({ rejectUnauthorized: false })
and additionally disables
checkServerIdentity
, so the client accepts:
Certificates signed by any CA (including self-signed),
Certificates whose
CN
/
SAN
does not match the hostname,
Expired certificates,
Certificates issued for a different hostname on the same IP.
It does not emit any log line, warning, or deprecation notice at runtime. The
openid-client
documentation explicitly labels the flag "for local development only" and warns that it is unsafe in production.
Who calls
getClient()
getClient()
is the single entry point for every OIDC operation in Immich. Every login that goes through OIDC reaches the attacker-controlled network path on first use. Every subsequent token refresh goes through the same code path. A network-adjacent attacker who can intercept even one OIDC handshake obtains a session for the victim.
Complementary sink —
getProfilePicture
fetch
server/src/repositories/oauth.repository.ts:116-126
:
async
getProfilePicture
(
url
:
string
)
{
const
response
=
await
fetch
(
url
)
;
...
}
This is Node's built-in
fetch
, not
openid-client
, so it does not inherit the
allowInsecureRequests
behaviour — but it is called with the raw
profile.picture
claim from the OIDC UserInfo response. An attacker who has already defeated the OIDC TLS handshake (via this advisory's primary finding) can return an arbitrary
picture
URL in the UserInfo response, and
getProfilePicture
will fetch it with no private-IP check, producing a secondary SSRF primitive reachable from the Immich server to its own internal network.
The SSRF sink itself is the same class as the Penpot
create-file-media-object-from-url
advisory submitted earlier this week (
GHSA-g928-c7qm-9w3q
).
Taint chain (MitM scenario)
Immich server          NetworkPath          Legitimate IdP
      │                 attacker              (Keycloak/Authentik/etc.)
      │◄─────discovery(new URL(issuerUrl))
      │                   │
      │                   ▼
      │       [attacker intercepts TLS, presents self-signed cert]
      │                   │
      │        [allowInsecureRequests accepts cert]
      │                   │
      │                   ▼
      │         crafted .well-known/openid-configuration
      │         {
      │           token_endpoint:    "https://attacker.example/token"
      │           userinfo_endpoint: "https://attacker.example/userinfo"
      │           jwks_uri:          "https://attacker.example/jwks"
      │         }
      ▼
  openid-client now calls attacker endpoints
      ▼
  /token → attacker returns forged id_token signed with attacker JWKS
      ▼
  /userinfo → attacker returns { sub: "admin@immich.example", email: "admin@immich.example" }
      ▼
  userMapping.findOrCreate(email) → matches local admin user
      ▼
  session issued for admin user → attacker logs in as admin
Exploitation scenarios
Network-adjacent MitM
(primary). An attacker on the same LAN as the Immich container, or on any network segment between the Immich pod and the IdP, intercepts the TCP connection and completes the TLS handshake with a self-signed cert. Requires
arp-spoof
/ rogue AP / VLAN hop. Affects every Immich deployment where the IdP is not on the same host.
Malicious egress proxy
. An organization that routes all Immich egress through an HTTPS-intercepting proxy (a common CASB pattern) unknowingly feeds Immich's
openid-client
a re-signed response for the IdP. Because Immich accepts any cert, the re-signed response is trusted even if the proxy itself is compromised.
Compromised DNS / BGP
. An attacker who can poison DNS for the IdP hostname (local DNS cache poisoning, BGP hijack) can redirect the TLS connection to their own server and present any cert. Without TLS verification, the redirection is undetectable.
Docker-compose with rogue sidecar
. A malicious sidecar container in the same docker-compose network as the Immich server can intercept outbound TLS to the IdP by rewriting the DNS resolver (via
--dns
) or presenting itself as a transparent TLS proxy. Because Immich accepts any cert, the sidecar's forged response is trusted.
In every scenario, the attacker's end state is a valid Immich session under an arbitrary OIDC identity, including the administrator.
Impact
Network-adjacent-attacker authentication bypass on every OIDC-enabled Immich deployment. CVSS 3.1
AV:A / AC:H / PR:N / UI:N / S:U / C:H / I:H / A:N
=
6.1 Medium
with adjacent network attack vector. Elevated to
8.1 High
in deployments where the IdP is routed through an untrusted transparent proxy (the "every-cert-accepted" case is effectively unauthenticated once the attacker controls the proxy).
The defect is
design-level
: the code passes
allowInsecureRequests
regardless of deployment class, which is documented by
openid-client
as development-only.
Remediation
Remove
allowInsecureRequests
from the
execute
array unconditionally.
The fix is a one-line deletion in
getClient()
:
return
await
discovery
(
new
URL
(
issuerUrl
)
,
clientId
,
metadata
,
authMethod
,
{
timeout
}
// <-- drop execute entirely
)
;
openid-client
defaults to strict TLS verification, which is the correct production behaviour.
Gate the flag behind an explicit opt-in env var for local development.
If operators need to point Immich at an IdP with a self-signed cert in a lab environment, require
OIDC_ALLOW_INSECURE_TLS=true
to engage the flag, and log a CRITICAL warning at boot when it is set. This preserves the developer-convenience path without shipping it as the default.
Enforce HTTPS-only
issuerUrl
at config save time.
Add
@IsUrl({protocols: ['https'], require_protocol: true})
to the
issuerUrl
field at
server/src/dtos/system-config.dto.ts:439-441
. The current
@IsString()
accepts
http://attacker.example/
, which is an orthogonal issue but compounds the TLS-verification defect.
Rotate OIDC-sourced sessions after the fix
. Any session currently logged in via OIDC may have been minted through an intercepted handshake. The fix-up release should force a re-login for all OIDC-authenticated users.
Regression test
. Add a Jest test in
server/src/repositories/oauth.repository.spec.ts
that constructs a
getClient()
call against a mock IdP with a self-signed cert and asserts the call rejects with a TLS error.
Timeline
2026-04-11 — defect identified via source review at HEAD;
allowInsecureRequests
is imported and unconditionally passed to
discovery()
; advisory drafted for submission via
security/advisories/new
.
Severity
Moderate
4.7
CVSS overall score
This score calculates overall vulnerability severity from 0 to 10 and is based on the Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS).
/ 10
CVSS v3 base metrics

Network
Attack complexity
High
Privileges required
None

Required
Scope
Changed
Confidentiality

Learn more about base metrics
CVSS v3 base metrics
Attack vector:
More severe the more the remote (logically and physically) an attacker can be in order to exploit the vulnerability.
Attack complexity:
More severe for the least complex attacks.
Privileges required:
More severe if no privileges are required.
User interaction:
More severe when no user interaction is required.
Scope:
More severe when a scope change occurs, e.g. one vulnerable component impacts resources in components beyond its security scope.
Confidentiality:
More severe when loss of data confidentiality is highest, measuring the level of data access available to an unauthorized user.
Integrity:
More severe when loss of data integrity is the highest, measuring the consequence of data modification possible by an unauthorized user.
Availability:
More severe when the loss of impacted component availability is highest.
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N
CVE ID
No known CVE
Weaknesses
Weakness
CWE-295
Improper Certificate Validation
The product does not validate, or incorrectly validates, a certificate.
Learn more on MITRE.
Credits

title: OIDC discovery/token/userinfo/JWKS fetches run with TLS certificate verification unconditionally disabled via allowInsecureRequests (CWE-295)
